children.text: The camp blends modern comfort with authentic Balinese design, creating spaces that complement the surf-focused lifestyle without pretension. Think functional elegance rather than resort excess—a place to recover between sessions, connect with surf buddies, and wake refreshed for dawn patrol.

Beautiful balinese gardens surround the property, providing peaceful interludes between the energy of surf sessions and social areas. Air conditioning keeps rooms comfortable in tropical heat, while open-air common spaces embrace the warm climate.
Room Options
Accommodation spans budget levels without sacrificing comfort:
Shared dorms
provide affordable beds for solo travelers and those prioritizing surf investment over luxury
Private rooms
offer personal space with quality bedding and modern amenities
Private bungalows
deliver additional seclusion surrounded by tropical gardens
Luxurious villas
feature premium finishes for guests wanting elevated comfort
All options include air conditioning or fan, secure storage for surf equipment, and easy access to camp facilities.
Camp Facilities

The pool serves as the social heart of the camp—perfect for cooling off, nursing tired muscles, or simply relaxing with new friends between sessions. A swimming pool this well-designed becomes essential for post-surf recovery in tropical heat.
Additional facilities include:
Open-air yoga studio hosting daily yoga sessions
Outdoor gym for maintaining strength and flexibility
Restaurant serving healthy meals with international flavors
Bar and lounge areas for evening socializing
Reliable WiFi throughout the property
BBQ and Pizza nights and live music create regular opportunities for the community to gather, share wave stories, and build connections that often outlast the trip itself.

Among our Bali’s surf camps, Padang Padang stands apart through its prime positioning on the Bukit Peninsula. Where other camps require drives to reach quality waves, this location delivers walking distance access to multiple world-class breaks.

The camp pioneered surf instruction on the Bukit, establishing operations in 2005 when the founder recognized the area’s unmatched potential. That 15+ year commitment has cultivated deep relationships with local surfers, intimate knowledge of every break’s nuances, and refined coaching methodology.

Compared to other Indonesian surf destinations—the Mentawais, Lombok, Sumatra—the Bukit offers superior consistency and variety. You’ll find waves every day of the year here, something few surf destinations can guarantee. And unlike boat-access breaks, you’ll immerse in authentic Balinese culture, traditions, and hospitality that provide an irreplaceable dimension beyond the surf itself.
For surf camp in uluwatu seekers, this location offers equivalent access to that legendary wave while providing more diverse options when conditions favor other breaks.

Daily rhythms blend surf, yoga, meals, and community into something greater than individual activities. Mornings begin early—dawn patrol rewards those willing to rise with glassy conditions and fewer crowds. Return to breakfast with new friends, discussing the session’s highlights over fresh fruit and strong coffee.

Mid-morning often means yoga classes or video analysis, followed by a second surf session as conditions allow. Afternoons invite pool time, exploration, or simply hanging in lounge areas watching surfers through binoculars at distant breaks.

The international community creates natural connections. Solo travelers find surf buddies within days. Couples share meals with fellow adventurers. The atmosphere encourages genuine interaction without forcing artificial socializing.

Respect for local Balinese Hindu culture permeates the experience. The camp integrates guests into island life rather than isolating them in a surf-only bubble, creating a memorable experience that extends beyond wave count.

children.text: The Bukit Peninsula rewards exploration beyond the lineup. Uluwatu Temple perches dramatically on cliffs above the break, hosting traditional Kecak fire dance performances at sunset—a genuinely moving cultural experience.

Local warungs serve fresh seafood at cliff-edge tables as stunning sunsets create the golden-hour scenes Bali is famous for. The laid-back beach bars of Bingin and Balangan offer afternoon cold drinks with ocean views.

Wellness options abound: traditional Balinese massage, spa treatments, and meditation complement the daily yoga offered at camp. The incredible surroundings—limestone cliffs, hidden beaches, temple ceremonies—provide constant visual reward for those who wander.

Night life ranges from mellow beachside gatherings to the more developed scene in Seminyak, an hour’s drive north. Most guests find the camp’s BBQ nights and live music sufficient entertainment after physically demanding surf sessions.

children.text: Costa Rica earned its reputation as Central America’s surfing capital through decades of delivering perfect waves to surfers from around the globe. The country’s unique geography creates two distinct coastlines—the Pacific coast with its powerful, consistent swells and the Caribbean side offering reef breaks during specific swell windows—ensuring rideable surf regardless of season.

What sets this destination apart extends far beyond the waves. The biodiversity surrounding every session is staggering: howler monkeys call from jungle canopies as you paddle out, scarlet macaws streak overhead between sets, and sea turtles occasionally share the lineup. The Pura Vida culture—that genuine warmth and laid-back philosophy Costa Ricans embody—transforms a simple surf vacation into something more meaningful.
Tamarindo established itself as the country’s original surf mecca in the 1970s, evolving from a remote fishing village into a world-renowned destination. Just south of town,
Playa Avellanas has developed into one of the region’s most beloved surf areas

, known for its relaxed atmosphere and variety of quality breaks spread along the coastline. Santa Teresa gained prominence in the 2000s for its uncrowded, versatile waves, while Nosara developed into a wellness-focused surf haven. These spots now host internationally recognized competitions and attract professionals training between tour events, cementing Costa Rica’s position among the world’s best surf destinations.

Costa Rica’s geography creates remarkably diverse surf conditions within short distances. Mountain ranges funnel offshore winds toward the coast, volcanic headlands shape point breaks, and river mouths deposit sandbars that shift with seasonal rains. Understanding these mechanics helps you maximize your time in the water during your surf camp Costa Rica experience.

The Pacific coast receives the most consistent swell activity, with groundswells traveling from the South Pacific and wind swells generated by local weather patterns. The Caribbean coast operates on a different cycle, lighting up when cold fronts push swell from the north during winter months.
Wave Type
Beach breaks dominate Costa Rica’s beginner-friendly zones, particularly around Tamarindo and
Playa Avellanas
, where shifting sandbars create mellow peaks perfect for learning proper technique. Avellanas is especially known for its variety of surf spots—ranging from playful beach breaks to the famous
Little Hawaii
, a powerful peak that comes alive on bigger swells.
These breaks offer both lefts and rights, allowing surfers to develop skills in both directions while enjoying uncrowded stretches of coastline.

Point breaks like the legendary Witch’s Rock and Ollie’s Point deliver long, peeling walls for advanced surfers seeking extended rides. Pavones, in the country’s remote southern zone, hosts the second-longest left point wave in the world—a dream destination for experienced surfers chasing perfection.

Reef breaks scattered along both coasts provide intermediate challenges with more predictable wave shapes. Santa Teresa features several reef-sand combinations that handle larger swells while maintaining workable conditions for progressing surfers, while
Avellanas offers multiple peaks along its long beach
, allowing surfers to find waves suited to different conditions and experience levels.
Beginners thrive on the soft sand breaks surrounding
Avellanas
, where gentle whitewater and forgiving conditions build confidence quickly. The mellow beach peaks across Playa Avellanas offer natural progression zones once pop-ups become consistent.
Intermediate surfers find ideal conditions at Nosara’s beach breaks, which deliver more power and longer walls for practicing cutbacks and bottom turns.
Avellanas also provides excellent intermediate waves
, particularly when mid-size swells create clean, rippable shoulders across the sandbars.

Advanced surfers target powerful reef breaks and remote point waves. Witch’s Rock and Ollie’s Point require boat access and deliver world-class surfing for those with the skills to handle serious power. On larger swells,
Little Hawaii in Avellanas produces heavy, fast waves
that challenge even experienced surfers.

The dry season from December through April delivers optimal Pacific coast conditions with consistent overhead swells, light winds, and sunny skies. Peak swell activity hits January through March, making this period ideal for surf camp life focused purely on wave riding.

The rainy season from May through November brings afternoon downpours but rarely impacts morning sessions. Swell consistency can drop during this period, though surprise swells still deliver excellent conditions. Many guests appreciate smaller crowds and lower prices during these months.
The Caribbean coast flips this pattern, receiving its best waves during December through March when cold fronts push swell into Puerto Viejo’s reef breaks.

Pacific coast swells arrive predominantly from the southwest and south, with wave heights typically ranging from chest-high to overhead during peak season. Larger swells occasionally push through, transforming mellow breaks into challenging conditions for advanced surfers only.

Offshore winds blow from the east during morning hours, grooming wave faces before onshore sea breezes kick in around midday. This pattern makes dawn patrol and early sessions the premium windows for glassy conditions.

The Central Valley mountain ranges create natural wind barriers that protect certain breaks from afternoon onshores. Santa Teresa benefits particularly from this geography, often maintaining clean conditions later than neighboring spots.

Tamarindo’s popularity means sharing waves during peak season, though its multiple breaks distribute surfers across considerable real estate. Early morning sessions before surf school crowds arrive offer the cleanest conditions and smallest lineups.

Surf camps provide significant advantages through guided access to less crowded spots. Many guests experience empty peaks at Playa Grande or Avellanas while tourists pack the main break. Experienced instructors know which breaks work best on specific tides and winds, maximizing quality wave counts.

Santa Teresa and Nosara maintain mellower lineups year-round, though holiday periods see increased numbers. Staying at beachfront location camps allows flexibility to surf during off-peak hours when most visitors eat or rest.

Our Costa Rica surf camps structure their programs around measurable progression rather than generic lessons. The best operations employ certified instructors with deep knowledge of local breaks, ocean dynamics, and teaching methodology that accelerates improvement regardless of starting point.

Small groups ensure personalized attention—the top camps maintain ratios as low as 6:1 student-to-coach. This approach means more waves, immediate feedback, and coaching customized to individual surfing skills rather than one-size-fits-all instruction.
Beginner Lessons

Daily surf sessions for beginners start with beach fundamentals: proper paddling technique, safe fall methods, and the mechanics of a successful pop-up. Instructors select gentle breaks with sandy bottoms and minimal currents, prioritizing safety while building confidence.

Ocean awareness education covers rip current identification, wave timing, and lineup etiquette—knowledge that keeps surfers safe for years beyond their camp experience. Many guests progress from whitewater rides to catching unbroken “green” waves within their first week.
Rapturecamps has refined beginner programs over decades, developing curriculum that consistently produces standing surfers within hours rather than days.
Intermediate Coaching

Surfers with basic skills focus on wave selection, positioning, and timing—the elements that separate occasional riders from real surfers. Coaches help students read incoming sets, identify the best takeoff zones, and generate speed through proper bottom turns.

Advanced maneuvers including cutbacks, floaters, and more aggressive turns build progressively as fundamentals solidify. Instructors push students to appropriate breaks where conditions match their developing abilities.

Sessions often include surf theory discussions covering wave forecasting, ocean physics, and equipment selection. Understanding the “why” behind techniques accelerates the “how” of executing them consistently.
Video Analysis
Video analysis transforms abstract feedback into concrete visual evidence. Underwater and land-based cameras capture sessions, with instructors reviewing footage during afternoon theory sessions.

Students see exactly where their timing breaks down, how body positioning affects speed generation, and what adjustments will unlock the next level. This feedback loop compresses learning curves that might otherwise take months.

Our surf camp programs integrate video review into weekly schedules rather than treating it as an optional add-on. Many guests describe seeing themselves surf for the first time as the breakthrough moment of their trip.

children.text: Our Costa Rica surf camp accommodations range from budget-conscious shared dorm setups to boutique private rooms. The common thread is a lifestyle-oriented approach where lodging enhances rather than distracts from the surf experience.
Tropical architecture dominates, with open-air common spaces that capture ocean breezes and facilitate the social connections that define surf camp life.
Room Options

Shared dorm rooms offer the most affordable entry point, typically housing 4-8 guests in bunk configurations with communal bathrooms. These options attract solo travelers seeking new friends and a social atmosphere.
Private rooms range from simple doubles to air-conditioned suites with amenities and ensuite bathrooms. Couples and those valuing quiet rest between sessions gravitate toward these options.
Family rooms and multi-bed configurations accommodate traveling groups, our camps are offering package discounts for larger bookings.
Camp Facilities
Pool areas serve as social hubs where many guests decompress between sessions, comparing wave counts and planning evening activities. Our surf camp features an infinity pool looking into the jungle.

Surf gear storage keeps boards protected and organized, with unlimited board rentals allowing guests to experiment with different shapes as their surfing skills develop. Equipment rooms typically include the perfect board for every condition and ability level.

Yoga decks host morning and afternoon yoga class sessions that complement surf training through flexibility work and breathwork. Recovery amenities including massage services help bodies bounce back for the next day’s waves.

Common areas with comfortable seating, charging stations, and WiFi create spaces where the international surf community connects. Many lifelong friendships begin during these between-session conversations.

Costa Rica stands apart from other Central American surf destinations through infrastructure, safety, and accessibility that simplify the vacation experience. Roads are paved to major surf zones, ATMs work reliably, and medical facilities meet international standards—logistics that matter when traveling with family or valuable camera equipment.

Political stability and a long tourism tradition mean welcoming locals who genuinely appreciate visitors. The Pura Vida philosophy isn’t marketing—it reflects a cultural commitment to enjoying life’s simple pleasures, surfing being chief among them.

Year-round warm water eliminates wetsuit hassles. You’ll paddle out in boardshorts and a surf shirt, feeling the tropical ocean directly rather than through neoprene. This comfort factor keeps surfers in the water longer and makes wipeouts pleasant rather than punishing.

The country’s compact size means a single trip can include multiple world-class zones. A few hours drive connects the airport to several distinct surf regions, each with unique character and wave types.

Daily rhythm at our Costa Rica surf camp follows natural patterns: early rises for dawn patrol sessions when offshore winds groom glassy faces, hearty breakfasts overlooking the ocean, and second sessions as conditions allow. Afternoons bring video analysis, yoga sessions, or adventure excursions before sunset cruise gatherings and group dinners.

The community atmosphere attracts solo travelers seeking connection alongside groups of friends and families sharing adventure. Mealtimes become spontaneous social events where surfers from different countries compare home breaks and plan future trips together.

Evening activities range from quiet nights by the pool to exploring skate parks close by or attending local cultural events. Camps organize group outings that introduce guests to authentic Costa Rican experiences beyond the beach.
Many guests arrive as strangers and depart with surf buddies scattered across the globe—connections forged through shared stoke in warm tropical waters.

children.text: Costa Rica delivers adventure beyond the lineup. Zip-lining through cloud forest canopies, hiking active volcanoes, and spotting wildlife in national parks fill non-surf hours with unforgettable experiences.

Hot springs near Arenal Volcano provide natural recovery after intense surf weeks. Wildlife watching tours reveal the biodiversity that makes this country extraordinary—sloths, toucans, monkeys, and countless other species inhabit jungles just minutes from the beach.

Local markets showcase traditional crafts and foods, while Spanish immersion programs at camps like School of the World add cultural depth to surf trip itineraries. Many guests extend their stays after discovering how much this beautiful country offers beyond its world class waves.
Explore Tamarindo’s vibrant town scene for shopping, dining, and nightlife, or venture to quieter zones where nature dominates. Costa Rica rewards curiosity in every direction.

children.text: Ericeira Portugal earned its designation as Europe’s first ever surf destination with good reason—it’s the only world surfing reserve on the European continent. This recognition places the coastline surrounding Ericeira alongside legendary surfing destinations like Malibu and Santa Cruz, cementing its status as Europe’s surf mecca.

The town itself remains an original fishing community, where weathered fishermen mend nets alongside surfers waxing boards. Cobblestone streets wind past whitewashed buildings adorned with blue trim, creating an authenticity that surf camps in more developed locations simply cannot replicate. This isn’t a purpose-built resort town—it’s a living, breathing Portuguese village that happens to sit atop some of the Atlantic Ocean’s most consistent waves.

Within just a few kilometers of the town center, surfers find an extraordinary concentration of quality surf spots. From the world-famous Ribeira d’Ilhas, which hosts international competitions, to the beautiful Coxos beach with its powerful barrels, the variety rivals surf areas anywhere on Earth. The density of excellent waves within such a compact stretch makes Ericeira uniquely efficient for surf sessions—you’ll spend more time in the water and less time driving between breaks.

The world surfing reserve Ericeira delivers remarkably consistent surf throughout the year, thanks to its exposure to North Atlantic swells and varied coastline geography. Unlike destinations that offer quality waves only during narrow seasonal windows, the Portuguese surf scene here provides options for every skill level across all twelve months.

The technical appeal lies in diversity. A single stretch of coast serves up everything from mellow beach breaks perfect for beginners to hollow reef breaks that challenge advanced surfers. This range means progression happens naturally—as skills develop, new breaks become accessible without traveling to different regions.
Wave Type

The coastline surrounding Ericeira features multiple break types within close proximity. Coxos stands as the crown jewel—a powerful right-hand reef break that produces the hollow barrels experienced surfers travel thousands of miles to ride. The beautiful Coxos beach itself is a pleasant horseshoe shaped beach, sheltered from the north wind and bathed in crystal-clear waters.

Beach breaks like Foz do Lizandro offer gentler, more forgiving waves that reform consistently, ideal for learning fundamental techniques. The wave faces here roll rather than pitch, giving beginners precious extra seconds to find their footing.

Point breaks and reef breaks dominate the more advanced surf spots, producing defined walls that allow for long, drawn-out turns. When larger swells arrive from the Atlantic Ocean, these waves transform into powerful, fast-moving walls that demand precision and commitment.

cardItems.label: Hazards
cardItems.value: Hard to get in and out, closeouts on larger swells

children.text: The range of surf spots accommodates everyone from complete beginners to professional competitors. Foz do Lizandro and São Julião provide soft, sandy-bottom breaks where new surfers can fall safely while mastering pop-ups and balance. The whitewater sections here remain manageable even on bigger days, offering protected learning zones.

Intermediate surfers find their progression grounds at Ribeira d’Ilhas and the various reef breaks north of town. These waves offer defined faces for practicing turns while remaining relatively forgiving compared to expert-only breaks.

More experienced surfers gravitate toward Coxos, Pedra Branca, and Reef. These powerful breaks demand confident paddling, precise positioning, and quick decision-making. When conditions align, they produce some of the best waves in Europe—but they’re not places for the underprepared.

September through May delivers the most consistent surf conditions, with autumn and spring offering the ideal balance of swell size, favorable winds, and comfortable water temperatures. Winter months bring larger, more powerful swells courtesy of North Atlantic storms, transforming intermediate breaks into expert-only zones while still offering protected options for developing surfers.

Summer brings smaller, cleaner waves and warmer water—excellent conditions for beginners and those seeking a relaxed surf holiday. The trade-off involves increased crowds as European vacationers descend on the Portuguese coast. However, the sheer number of breaks means finding uncrowded waves remains possible with local knowledge.
Ericeira enjoys 300+ days of sunshine annually, meaning weather rarely interrupts surf sessions regardless of season. Even winter days often feature clear skies between passing fronts.

The Atlantic Ocean delivers reliable swell energy from multiple directions, though northwest swells provide the most consistent quality. Storms tracking across the North Atlantic generate long-period groundswells that arrive with power and organization, creating the clean, defined waves Ericeira is known for.

Offshore winds (blowing from land toward sea) groom wave faces into glassy perfection. Morning hours typically offer the lightest winds, making dawn patrols particularly rewarding. The rocky cliffs and headlands create wind shadows that protect certain breaks even when afternoon breezes pick up.

The north wind, specifically, is blocked by coastal geography at several key breaks, including the beautiful Coxos beach. This sheltering effect means surfable conditions exist somewhere along the coastline on nearly any given day.

As Portugal’s premier surf destination, Ericeira does attract crowds—particularly at famous breaks during optimal conditions. Ribeira d’Ilhas and Coxos can become competitive when world-class waves coincide with weekends or competition periods.

Staying at a surf villa with local knowledge provides distinct advantages. Surf instructors and guides know the secondary breaks that remain uncrowded even when primary spots fill up. Early morning surf sessions, before day-trippers arrive from Lisbon, offer uncrowded conditions at most breaks. The ability to check multiple spots quickly from a base within walking distance means finding less-pressured peaks becomes straightforward.
The culture in the water remains generally friendly, though respecting lineup etiquette proves essential at the more serious breaks.

The Rapturecamps Coxos surf villa approach to development goes far beyond simply putting guests in the water. Structured progression forms the foundation—each surfer receives assessment, targeted coaching, and measurable feedback designed to accelerate improvement.

Experienced surf instructors bring decades of combined water time and formal coaching credentials. They understand not just how to surf, but how to teach surfing—different skills entirely. Sessions are structured around specific objectives rather than aimless paddling, ensuring every surf session moves guests closer to their goals.

The methodology combines time-tested fundamentals with modern training techniques. Theory sessions, land-based drills, and targeted water practice work together to build muscle memory and wave-reading instincts that persist long after the surf trip ends.
Beginner Lessons

New surfers begin in controlled environments at sandy beach breaks where the consequences of mistakes remain minimal. Surf lessons start on land, covering ocean safety, board handling, paddling technique, and the mechanics of standing up. Only after demonstrating competence with fundamentals do beginners enter the water.

Initial surf sessions take place in waist-deep whitewater, removing the complexity of wave-catching while allowing full focus on the pop-up motion. Group sizes stay small—typically four students maximum per instructor—ensuring personalized attention and immediate feedback.

Progression follows a careful sequence: riding whitewater prone, then kneeling, then standing. Once standing becomes consistent, the focus shifts to steering and eventually catching unbroken green waves. This methodical approach builds genuine competence rather than lucky one-off successes.
Intermediate Coaching

Surfers who can already catch green waves and ride along the face benefit from refinement-focused coaching. Sessions analyze and improve bottom turns, top turns, cutbacks, and the subtle weight shifts that transform awkward movements into flowing maneuvers.

Wave selection receives significant attention. Learning to read swells before they break, identifying which waves offer the best opportunity, and positioning correctly in the lineup separates intermediate surfers from advanced practitioners. Coaches accompany guests into the lineup, providing real-time guidance on when to paddle and where to sit.

The progression pathway eventually leads from friendly beach breaks to more challenging reef breaks. This transition happens gradually, with coaches selecting conditions appropriate to each surfer’s developing abilities.
Video Analysis

Modern coaching accelerates dramatically when surfers can see themselves objectively. Filming from the beach captures positioning, timing, technique, and wave selection—all elements difficult to self-assess while actually surfing.

Review sessions happen on the villa’s terrace or in comfortable common spaces, with coaches pausing footage to highlight specific moments. Side-by-side comparisons with professional footage illustrate the differences between current technique and target form.

The feedback proves specific and actionable: paddle position during take-off, head movement during turns, weight distribution through maneuvers. Surfers leave with clear mental images of what to work on during subsequent surf sessions.

children.text: The modern surf villa sits perched above the coastline, offering a breathtaking sea view that makes checking conditions as simple as glancing out the window. Contemporary design merges with surf lifestyle sensibility—clean lines, natural light flooding through oversized windows, and seamless transitions between indoor comfort and outdoor terraces.

The spacious villa was designed with active travelers in mind. Surfboards stow conveniently near entrances. Outdoor showers rinse salt before entering living spaces. The terrace and pool provide recovery zones where tired muscles regenerate while watching the sunset paint the Atlantic Ocean in shades of orange and pink.

Good vibes permeate the common areas, where travelers from around the world share wave stories over delicious breakfast or cold drinks. The atmosphere balances social energy with private retreat—conversation flows easily but personal space remains abundant.
Room Options

Accommodation options range from private double rooms to shared spaces offering excellent value. Many rooms feature ocean views, allowing guests to wake to the sight of morning sets rolling toward shore.
Private suites on upper floors provide the most spacious villa experience, with some including private balconies overlooking the water.

Shared rooms maintain high comfort standards while creating opportunities to meet fellow travelers. Clean, comfortable beds, individual storage, and access to all villa facilities make these options attractive for solo travelers prioritizing experience over privacy.
Villa Facilities

The pool directly facing the beautiful Portuguese Sea serves as the villa’s centerpiece. After morning surf sessions, there’s nothing quite like a refreshing dip in the heated saltwater pool while watching other surfers catch waves below.

Common areas include a light-filled kitchen, comfortable lounges, and outdoor terraces designed for socializing. Fast and free wifi keeps guests connected, while designated quiet zones accommodate those needing focused work time.

Additional amenities support the surf lifestyle comprehensively: secure board storage, wetsuit drying areas, rental bikes for exploring town, and a board test center where guests can experiment with different shapes and sizes. The integration of these features creates an environment where surfing remains the effortless focus of each day.

Among Portugal’s many surfing destinations, Ericeira stands apart for several compelling reasons. The world surfing reserve designation isn’t marketing—it’s official recognition of exceptional wave quality and environmental protection. Only a handful of locations worldwide have earned this status.

Proximity to Lisbon provides practical advantages unmatched by more remote surf areas. International flights arrive daily at Lisbon’s airport, with the coastline reachable in under an hour. This accessibility makes Ericeira practical for shorter surf trips without sacrificing wave quality.

The concentration of surf spots within a compact area maximizes water time. Rather than driving hours between breaks as required in spread-out surf regions, surfers here access a dozen quality options within minutes. This density means conditions somewhere are nearly always suitable.

Beyond waves, Ericeira retains authentic Portuguese character increasingly rare along tourist-developed coastlines. The Portuguese surf scene here grew organically within an existing community rather than overwhelming it. Visitors experience genuine local cuisine, legitimate cultural traditions, and a town that functions for residents, not just tourists.

The #rapturefamily philosophy extends to every guest from arrival to departure. This isn’t simply accommodation—it’s entry into a global community of surf enthusiasts united by shared passion for wave riding and adventure travel.

Social programming creates connections naturally. Sunset BBQs and Pizza nights bring guests together twice weekly, transforming strangers into travel friends over grilled fresh seafood and local wine. Weekly wine tastings introduce Portuguese varieties while sparking conversations that continue into the city’s vibrant nightlife.

The atmosphere welcomes solo travellers and groups equally. Traveling alone? You’ll meet companions for surf sessions, dinner outings, and day trips within hours of arrival. Traveling with friends? The communal spaces enhance your existing bonds while introducing you to fellow adventurers from around the world.

Staff embody this welcoming spirit, treating guests as friends rather than customers. Their local knowledge extends beyond waves to restaurant recommendations, hidden beaches, and the cultural experiences that transform a surf holiday into lasting memories.

What Our Guest Say About Ericeira

children.text: Ericeira earned its reputation as Europe’s surf capital through decades of dedicated wave riding that began when international travelers discovered these powerful Atlantic waves in the late 1960s. The town’s transformation from quiet fishing village to surf mecca culminated in 2011 when the Save The Waves Coalition designated Ericeira as the first World Surfing Reserve in Europe—a recognition that protects this exceptional surf ecosystem for future generations.

The coastline here tells a story written by the Atlantic Ocean over millennia. Rocky cliffs drop into crystal-clear waters where limestone reefs shape world-class waves, while sandy beaches offer gentler breaks for those learning the craft. Local fishermen—considered the area’s original surfers for their wave-riding boats—still bless surf events, connecting modern surf culture with centuries of maritime heritage.

Walking through town reveals the perfect balance between old and new. Traditional whitewashed houses line narrow cobbled streets where surf shops now sit alongside family-run restaurants serving the morning catch. The Ericeira Surf Clube, established in 1993 by local pioneers, continues shaping the community through events, coaching, and coastal protection advocacy.

Ericeira’s coastline stretches approximately 10km of diverse surf breaks exposed to consistent swell from the open Atlantic. This geographical positioning creates rarely flat conditions, with waves arriving from multiple directions throughout the year. The variety of breaks means surfers can find excellent waves regardless of conditions, moving between spots as tides, swells, and winds shift.
Wave Type

The coast features an impressive mix of wave types that cater to every surfing style. Legendary reef breaks like Coxos and Pedra Branca produce powerful, barreling waves over limestone formations—these world famous breaks draw advanced surfers seeking challenging conditions. Point breaks at Ribeira d’Ilhas offer longer, more workable walls ideal for progression, while sandy beach breaks at Foz do Lizandro provide forgiving waves perfect for beginners.

Both right and left-hand breaks exist within short distances of each other. Wave sizes range from playful 1-foot summer ripples to thundering 15-foot winter swells. The limestone reef formations create predictable takeoff zones and clean wave faces that respond beautifully to different swell angles.

children.text: Ericeira welcomes all skill levels through its remarkable break diversity:
Beginner-friendly breaks

like Foz do Lizandro and Praia do Sul feature sandy bottoms, gentle waves, and ample space for learning without hazards. These beaches provide ideal conditions for surf lessons and building fundamental skills.
Intermediate surfers
progress to breaks like Ribeira d’Ilhas and Praia do Norte, where more powerful walls allow practice of cutbacks, bottom turns, and wave selection in dynamic conditions.
Advanced surfers
find their challenges at Coxos, Pedra Branca, and Cave—heavy reef breaks demanding experience, fitness, and respect for the ocean’s power.
This concentration of breaks across skill levels within walking distance of town makes Ericeira exceptional for structured progression.
Summer (May-September)

brings smaller, cleaner waves and warm weather—ideal conditions for beginners and those seeking a relaxed surf holiday. Water temperatures reach 18°C, crowds increase, but the fun atmosphere and gentle waves create perfect learning environments.
Winter (October-April)

delivers powerful Atlantic swells that light up the reef breaks. Experienced surfers chase these larger waves, with Coxos producing some of Europe’s best barrels. Water drops to around 15°C, requiring thicker wetsuits.
Shoulder seasons (April-May and September-October)
offer the best waves for all levels—warm enough for comfort, powerful enough for progression, and quieter than peak summer.

West to northwest Atlantic swells provide the most consistent wave production, arriving with remarkable regularity throughout the year. This consistent swell window means surfers rarely face flat spells, making Ericeira an ideal surf trip destination regardless of timing.

Northeast winds create offshore conditions at most breaks, grooming wave faces and holding up lips for clean riding. The Sintra mountains inland influence local wind patterns, often creating lighter morning breezes before afternoon sea breezes develop.

Tidal movements significantly affect wave quality at different breaks. Low tide exposes reefs for more powerful waves, while higher tides soften conditions at beach breaks—local knowledge proves invaluable for timing sessions correctly.

Popular breaks like Coxos and Ribeira d’Ilhas attract surfers from around Europe, creating competition for waves during peak seasons. However, the sheer number of surf spots—over 65 breaks—naturally disperses crowds across the coastline.

Staying at our surfcamp in Ericeira provides advantages: surf instructors with local knowledge direct guests toward lesser-known spots producing excellent waves without the crowds. Early morning and late afternoon sessions also offer quieter lineups as day-trippers depart.
Surf lessons bookable with our surf packages ensure guests maximize wave count while respecting lineup etiquette and local surfers.

A structured approach to improvement defines our quality standards at our Ericeira surfcamps, leveraging the area’s ideal learning environment across varied breaks. Certified surf instructors combine Portuguese surf school traditions with international coaching methodologies, creating personalized progression pathways for every guest.
Beginner Lessons

Surf classes for beginners start at safe, sandy breaks like Foz do Lizandro where forgiving waves and sandy bottoms minimize risk. Beach theory sessions cover ocean safety, wave reading, and surfing etiquette before entering the water.
Small groups receive focused attention on fundamental skills:
Proper paddling technique for efficient wave catching
Pop-up mechanics for reliable standing
Balance and weight distribution on the board
Basic wave selection for successful rides
Surf equipment suited to beginners—soft-top longboards and properly fitted wetsuits—ensures comfort and stability throughout the learning process.
Intermediate Coaching

Intermediate surfers progress to more challenging breaks like Ribeira d’Ilhas, where more powerful waves demand refined technique. Coaches focus on reading wave sections, positioning for optimal takeoffs, and generating speed through proper board control.
Skill development expands to include:
Bottom turns that set up maneuvers
Cutbacks for returning to the power source
Top turns on steeper wave faces
Adapting to different wave types and conditions
Surf guiding introduces intermediate surfers to breaks matching their developing abilities, building confidence across varied conditions.
Video Analysis
GoPro footage captures sessions for detailed technical review—an accelerated path to improvement. Coaches break down each wave, analyzing:
Body positioning during turns
Timing of maneuvers relative to wave sections
Paddle efficiency and wave selection choices
Comparison with professional technique
Personalized improvement plans emerge from video review, giving surfers clear objectives for subsequent sessions. This feedback loop transforms a week of surfing into months of typical progression.

Proximity to Lisbon opens cultural possibilities—day trips to the vibrant capital or historic Sintra palaces complement surf-focused schedules. Yet Ericeira’s consistent waves mean no need to travel far for excellent surf.

children.text: The Alentejo region remains Portugal’s hidden surf gem precisely because most travelers drive past it toward Ericeira or the Algarve. Their loss becomes your gain. The coastline stretches through the Costa Vicentina, a protected natural park where development stays minimal and the ocean keeps its wild character.

Villages like Vila Nova de Milfontes, Porto Covo, and São Torpes maintain their fishing heritage while welcoming surfers who appreciate authenticity over nightclubs. The pace here matches the landscape—unhurried, unpretentious, grounded in tradition. You’ll hear Portuguese spoken at the local café, see nets drying in the sun, and feel the Atlantic’s presence in everything.

This isn’t the Portugal of Instagram influencers and rooftop bars. This is the Portugal where vineyards roll toward clifftops, where dinner means grilled fish and local wine, where the word “crowds” simply doesn’t apply. For surfers willing to venture slightly off the beaten track, Alentejo delivers an experience the busier regions cannot replicate.

Alentejo’s coastline faces the full force of Atlantic swells while offering enough geographical variation to create diverse conditions. The region catches northwest swells consistently, with beach breaks, point breaks, and reef setups scattered along the coast. This variety means conditions exist for multiple surf levels on any given day.
Wave Type

The Alentejo coast features predominantly beach breaks with sandy bottoms, making it forgiving for beginners while still providing fun waves for progressing surfers. Spots like Malhão deliver classic beach break peaks with both rights and lefts. Aivados offers more power when swells increase. Ilha do Pessegueiro provides a scenic setup opposite a small island where waves refract around the landmass.

On smaller swells, waves break gently with soft shoulders—ideal for practicing turns and building confidence. When larger Atlantic systems arrive, particularly in autumn and winter, the same spots transform into more challenging peaks with steeper faces and faster sections. This flexibility makes the region work across seasons and abilities.

cardItems.label: Hazards
cardItems.value: Hard to get in and out, closeouts on larger swells

children.text: Beginners find Alentejo almost purpose-built for learning. Summer brings mellow, waist-high conditions where mistakes carry minimal consequences. The sandy bottoms forgive wipeouts, and the lack of crowds means you can practice without dodging other surfers.

Intermediate surfers discover excellent progression opportunities during shoulder seasons when swells increase but remain manageable. Spots like Porto Covo and Praia do Carvalhal offer walls suitable for working on cutbacks and generating speed. Advanced surfers may find summer months too mellow, though autumn can deliver overhead conditions at exposed breaks.

March through October represents the primary surf season for our Alentejo surf camps. Summer months (June through August) bring smaller, cleaner swells perfect for beginners and those prioritizing sun alongside surfing. Water temperatures warm enough for short wetsuits make sessions comfortable.

Spring and autumn deliver the sweet spot—larger swells, fewer visitors, and pleasant weather both in and out of the water. September and October particularly shine, combining residual summer warmth with increased Atlantic activity. Winter brings powerful swells but also storms, cold water, and conditions better suited for experienced surfers than those on a learning-focused surf trip.

The coast receives primary swells from the northwest, which the Atlantic Ocean generates consistently. Secondary swells from the west and southwest also reach these beaches, providing variety in wave direction. The coastline’s orientation means different spots favor different swell angles.

Morning sessions typically enjoy light offshore conditions before thermal winds develop. The Portuguese trade winds—called “Nortada”—blow from the north during summer afternoons, making early surf sessions the priority. Protected bays and coves offer shelter when winds increase, and experienced surf schools know where to find clean conditions throughout the day.

This section could be summarized in three words: there are none. Compared to Peniche, Ericeira, or the Algarve’s popular breaks, Alentejo lineups feel empty. Even during August, you might share a peak with a handful of other surfers rather than fighting for scraps among dozens.

Surf camps in the area compound this advantage by knowing secondary spots away from the few visitors who do arrive. Our surf school takes you where conditions align with your surf level, often to beaches you’d never find independently. The luxury of uncrowded waves means more practice, faster progression, and a pure surf experience uninterrupted by lineup politics.

Our Alentejo surfcamps approach improvement systematically rather than simply providing surf lessons and hoping for the best. The combination of uncrowded conditions, consistent waves, and qualified instruction creates an environment where progression happens naturally.
Beginner Lessons

Your first day introduces fundamentals on the beach before you touch water. Instructors cover paddling technique, the pop-up movement, positioning on the board, and basic ocean awareness. Safety education includes reading currents, understanding wave patterns, and respecting the ocean’s power.

Once in the water, small groups ensure individual attention. The mellow Alentejo waves give beginners time to practice each component without the stress of larger, faster surf. Most guests stand up and ride whitewater within their first session, progressing to catching unbroken waves before the full week concludes.
Intermediate Coaching

Surfers with foundational skills focus on refinement. Coaches analyze wave selection—reading sets, identifying peaks, positioning for optimal takeoffs. Technique work addresses common plateaus: generating speed through proper body positioning, initiating turns from the correct part of the wave, reading sections to plan maneuvers.

The uncrowded conditions prove particularly valuable here. Intermediates need repetition to build muscle memory, and catching multiple waves per session accelerates learning dramatically compared to crowded lineups where you fight for every ride.
Video Analysis

Seeing yourself surf transforms understanding. Video feedback sessions capture your sessions, then coaches break down footage frame by frame. You’ll see exactly where your stance narrows on turns, when your timing lags on takeoffs, how your positioning affects wave selection.

This objective feedback shortcuts months of trial-and-error. The combination of water time and visual analysis helps guests internalize corrections quickly, carrying improvements into subsequent sessions rather than repeating the same mistakes.

children.text: Our surf house in Alentejo delivers accommodation that matches the regional character—authentic, comfortable, and connected to the landscape. We are not a generic hotel. Room Options

At our surfcamp in Milfontes, we offer a range of accommodation options to match different travel styles. Shared rooms are perfect for solo travelers and guests who enjoy the social side of a surf trip, while our glamping tents provide extra comfort and space for couples or anyone who prefers more privacy.
Surrounded by nature and close to both the beaches and the town of Vila Nova de Milfontes, our camp combines convenience with a relaxed coastal atmosphere.

All rooms are clean, comfortable, and designed with surfers in mind. You’ll have space to store your gear, dry wetsuits after long sessions, and fully recharge with a good night’s sleep — so you’re ready for the next day of surfing in Alentejo.
Camp Facilities

The common areas are the social heart of our surfcamp in Milfontes. Spacious outdoor terraces, relaxed lounge areas, and shared dining spaces create the perfect setting to unwind after a day of surfing in Alentejo.

Surrounded by nature and fresh Atlantic air, our camp offers plenty of space to slow down — whether that’s stretching out in the sun, reading in a quiet corner, or connecting with other surfers over a shared meal.

We provide secure storage for surf equipment, so your boards and wetsuits are safely organized between sessions. Comfortable evening spaces invite long dinners, BBQ nights, and relaxed conversations — the moments where surf trips turn into lasting friendships.
WiFi is available if you need to stay connected, but most guests quickly find themselves switching into the slower rhythm that makes Milfontes and the Alentejo coast so special.

Alentejo distinguishes itself from Portugal’s other surf regions through authenticity rather than intensity. Where Ericeira delivers world-class waves amid increasing crowds, Alentejo offers great waves in peaceful solitude. Where the Algarve caters to package tourists and party seekers, Alentejo attracts those wanting genuine Portuguese atmosphere.

The beginner-friendly conditions make this region ideal for first-timers and improvers. The lack of localism means relaxed sessions where learning takes priority over competition. The connection to traditional Portugal—fishing villages, vineyard-covered hills, unhurried rhythms—creates a surfing holiday that feeds more than just your wave count.
For surfers prioritizing progression over proving themselves in heavy conditions, Alentejo represents the best surf environment in Portugal.

Daily rhythm in Alentejo follows natural patterns. Early mornings start with breakfast before the first session while winds stay light and lineups empty. Surf courses fill the peak hours, with instructors adapting locations based on conditions and group needs.
Afternoons invite exploration—walking trails along dramatic cliffs, visiting nearby villages, recovering with yoga in camp gardens. The environment encourages both activity and genuine rest.

Evenings bring communal dinner, often featuring fresh local food and Portuguese wine. BBQs happen regularly, creating space where guests meet people from around the world while sharing stories from the day’s waves. The darkness here reveals stars invisible in light-polluted cities—proper darkness that reminds you how far you’ve traveled from ordinary life.

New friends emerge naturally. The shared experience of learning, wiping out, succeeding, and recovering builds connections faster than everyday social situations. Many guests return specifically for this atmosphere, finding the perfect combination of surfing, nature, and community.

children.text: The Alentejo region offers depth beyond its waves. Traditional villages maintain centuries-old architecture and customs. Local cuisine showcases regional specialties—fresh seafood, black pork, bread soups, wines from nearby vineyards. Activities complement surfing naturally. The Costa Vicentina provides hiking trails along dramatic clifftops with Atlantic views. Horseback riding covers landscapes inaccessible by car. Wine tasting introduces Alentejo’s growing reputation among European wine regions. Cork forests—Portugal produces most of the world’s cork—create unusual landscapes worth exploring.

Day trips reach Lisbon in approximately two hours if city culture calls. More often, guests find enough richness locally that leaving feels unnecessary. What Our Guest Say About Alentejo

children.text: Ericeira trägt seit Oktober 2011 den Titel World Surfing Reserve – als erste und einzige Region Europas mit dieser Auszeichnung. Dieser Status wurde der Küste verliehen, weil sich hier auf etwa vier bis acht Kilometern mindestens sieben außergewöhnliche Surfspots konzentrieren: Ribeira d’Ilhas, Coxos, Cave, Pedra Branca, Crazy Left und São Lourenço bieten ein Spektrum von sanften Beachbreaks bis zu weltklasse Reefbreaks.

Die Lage unserer Surf Villa ermöglicht dir schnellen Zugang zu all diesen Spots. Von entspannten Wellen für Anfänger bei Foz do Lizandro bis zu den kraftvollen Barrels von Coxos – hier findest du jeden Tag die passende Welle. Die Kombination aus konsistenten Swells, vielfältigen Wellentypen und einer tief verwurzelten Surfkultur macht Ericeira zu einem Ort, der Surfer aus der ganzen Welt anzieht. Bei uns erlebst du diese einzigartige Surf-Destination hautnah.

Als Gastgeber in Ericeiras Herzen kennen wir die lokalen Surfbedingungen genau. Die Küste vor unserer Villa bietet eine seltene Kombination aus verschiedenen Break-Typen und Ausrichtungen, sodass bei nahezu jeder Swell- und Windkombination gute Wellen zu finden sind.
Wellentyp

Rund um unsere Surf Villa findest du sowohl Beach- als auch Reefbreaks. Ribeira d’Ilhas ist ein klassischer Pointbreak mit langen rechten Wellen und steilen Abschnitten nahe den Felsen – perfekt für Progressive Lines und konstantes Surfen. Coxos hingegen ist ein Power-Reefbreak mit heftigen Barrels und langen Perioden, der bei großen Swells seine volle Kraft entfaltet. São Julião und Foz do Lizandro bieten breite Beachbreaks mit vielen Peaks, die bei kleineren Swells ideal funktionieren.

cardItems.label: Hazards
cardItems.value: Hard to get in and out, closeouts on larger swells

children.text: Die Vielfalt der Spots macht unsere Lage für alle Könnensstufen attraktiv. Anfänger finden in den Beachbreaks von São Julião und Foz do Lizandro sichere Bedingungen mit sandigen Untergründen. Fortgeschrittene Surfer können sich an den technisch anspruchsvolleren Abschnitten von Ribeira d’Ilhas verbessern. Breaks wie Coxos, Cave und Crazy Left sind ausschließlich für erfahrene Surfer geeignet – hier brechen die Wellen auf sehr niedrigem Riff, oft hohl und kraftvoll.

Die besten Surfbedingungen in Ericeira herrschen von Oktober bis März, wenn konsistente Groundswells aus Nord bis Nordwest die Küste erreichen. In dieser Zeit liegen die durchschnittlichen Wellenhöhen zwischen 1,8 und 2,2 Metern. Die Sommermonate bieten kleinere Wellen (1,1 bis 1,7 Meter) mit mehr Windswells – ideal für Anfänger und entspanntere Sessions. Ganzjährig findest du surfbare Bedingungen, wobei Frühjahr und Herbst oft die perfekte Balance aus Swellkonsistenz und angenehmem Wetter bieten.

Die optimale Swellrichtung für die Spots rund um unsere Villa liegt zwischen Nord und Nordwest. Offshore-Winde aus Osten oder Nordosten schaffen die besten Bedingungen und glätten die Wellenoberfläche. Die steilen Klippen und Buchten entlang der Küste modulieren den Wind, sodass einzelne Breaks bei unterschiedlichen Windrichtungen geschützt sind. Im Winter dominieren Groundswells zu etwa 84 bis 91 Prozent – Wellen, die durch Stürme weit draußen auf dem Atlantik entstehen und mit Kraft und Qualität an der Küste ankommen.

Ericeiras Nähe zu Lissabon (nur 35 Kilometer) zieht an Wochenenden viele lokale Surfer an. Ribeira d’Ilhas und Foz do Lizandro können dann stark frequentiert sein. Als Gäste unserer Surf Villa profitiert ihr von lokaler Spotkenntnis: Wir wissen, welche Breaks zu welcher Zeit weniger crowded sind. Frühe Morgenstunden und Wochentage bieten deutlich mehr Platz im Wasser. In der Nebensaison – besonders im Frühjahr und Herbst – sind die Lineups spürbar leerer.
Unser strukturierter Coaching-Ansatz in der Surf Villa in
Ericeira

legt den Fokus auf echte, messbare Fortschritte – nicht nur darauf, Zeit im Wasser zu verbringen. Unsere erfahrenen lokalen Surflehrer kennen die Wellen rund um Ericeira seit vielen Jahren und kombinieren dieses Wissen mit modernen Coaching-Methoden, um jedem Gast eine individuelle Entwicklung zu ermöglichen.

Zu Beginn erhält jeder Surfer eine kurze Analyse seines aktuellen Levels. Darauf aufbauend setzen wir klare Ziele für die kommenden Tage im Wasser. Das Coaching passt sich anschließend täglich an – abhängig von den Surfbedingungen, deinem persönlichen Fortschritt und den Fähigkeiten, an denen du gerade arbeitest.
Die Vielzahl an Surfspots rund um Ericeira – von sanften Beachbreaks bis zu weltbekannten Reefbreaks – ermöglicht es uns, für jedes Level den perfekten Spot und die passenden Bedingungen zu wählen.
Anfänger-Surfkurs
Dein Einstieg ins Surfen beginnt bei uns an sicheren
Beachbreaks rund um Ericeira
, wo sandiger Untergrund ideale Bedingungen für die ersten Versuche bietet. Unsere erfahrenen Surflehrer begleiten dich Schritt für Schritt durch die Grundlagen des Surfens.

Der Kurs startet meist an Land mit einer Einführung in Ozeansicherheit, Paddeltechnik, Take-off und Balance auf dem Board. Anschließend geht es ins Weißwasser, wo du dich zunächst ganz auf das Aufstehen auf dem Brett konzentrieren kannst.

Mit kleinen Gruppen und viel persönlichem Feedback lernst du schnell, deine ersten Wellen zu reiten. Sobald das Aufstehen sicher funktioniert, arbeiten wir daran, Wellen selbstständig anzupaddeln und erste grüne Wellen zu surfen.
Coaching für Intermediates

Wenn du bereits grüne Wellen surfst, richtet sich unser Coaching gezielt auf die Weiterentwicklung deiner Technik. Gemeinsam arbeiten wir an wichtigen Manövern wie Bottom Turns, Top Turns und Cutbacks sowie an der richtigen Positionierung im Line-up.
Ein zentraler Bestandteil ist das
Lesen der Wellen

– zu verstehen, welche Wellen die besten Chancen bieten und wo du dich im Line-up positionieren musst. Unsere Coaches begleiten dich im Wasser und geben dir direktes Feedback zu Timing, Paddeltechnik und Linienwahl auf der Welle.
Dank der großen Auswahl an Spots im
Ericeira World Surfing Reserve
können wir unterschiedliche Wellenarten nutzen, um deine Fähigkeiten Schritt für Schritt weiterzuentwickeln.
Videoanalyse
Ein besonders effektives Tool für schnellen Fortschritt ist unsere
Videoanalyse
. Während der Sessions werden regelmäßig Fotos und Videos aufgenommen, sodass du deine Technik später aus einer objektiven Perspektive sehen kannst.
Gemeinsam mit unseren Coaches analysierst du deine Wellen: Position im Line-up, Timing beim Take-off, Körperhaltung während der Turns und deine Linienwahl auf der Welle.

Diese Analyse hilft dir, typische Fehler schneller zu erkennen und gezielt daran zu arbeiten. Mit klaren, konkreten Tipps gehst du anschließend in die nächste Session – und kannst die Verbesserungen direkt im Wasser umsetzen.

children.text: Unsere Surf Villa verbindet den authentischen Charme einer portugiesischen Architektur mit modernem Komfort. Nach einer intensiven Session erwartet dich hier ein Ort der Entspannung – vom Pool mit Meerblick bis zur großzügigen Terrasse unter Pinien. Hochwertige Materialien, durchdachte Einrichtung und eine entspannte Surfer Atmosphäre prägen jeden Raum.
Zimmeroptionen
Premium Suites
– Großzügige Suiten mit privatem Balkon, direktem Meerblick und luxuriöser Ausstattung und Premium-Bettwäsche sorgen für erholsame Nächte.
Deluxe Doppelzimmer
– Komfortable Doppelzimmer, hochwertigem Interieur und viel natürlichem Licht. Perfekt für Paare und Reisende mit gehobenem Anspruch.
Standard Doppelzimmer
– Gemütliche Zimmer mit Doppel- oder Einzelbetten, und allem Komfort für entspannte Surf Trips.
Mehrbettzimmer
– Ideal für Freundesgruppen oder Solo-Reisende, die eine Community-Atmosphäre schätzen. Gemeinsames Bad, hochwertige Ausstattung.
Villa-Ausstattung
Pool mit Meerblick
– Infinity-Feeling mit Sonnenliegen und direktem Blick auf den Atlantik
Holzterrasse und Garten
– Über 2000 m² Außenfläche zum Entspannen unter Pinien
Offene Küche und Wohnbereich
– Großzügiger Gemeinschaftsbereich mit modernem Stil
WLAN und Arbeitsplätze
– Perfekt für Digital Nomaden
Refill Station
– Nachhaltigkeit ist Teil unserer Philosophie

Ericeira ist nicht einfach nur ein Surfspot – es ist Europas einzige World Surfing Reserve und damit ein geschütztes Surfjuwel von internationaler Bedeutung. Unsere Surf Villa liegt im Herzen dieser besonderen Region, wo traditionelle Fischerkultur auf internationalen Surf Spirit trifft.

Die Vielfalt der Wellen macht Ericeira einzigartig: Vom perfekten Pointbreak bis zum herausfordernden Reef-Slab – alles innerhalb weniger Fahrminuten. Anders als in überlaufenen Surfdestinationen bewahrt Ericeira seinen authentischen Charakter. Die weiß getünchten Häuser, engen Gassen und familiengeführten Restaurants schaffen eine Atmosphäre, die du in keinem Resort der Welt findest.
Unsere Lage verbindet das Beste: Nähe zum Zentrum, schneller Zugang zu den Stränden und die Ruhe einer exklusiven Villa mit Meerblick.

pageBuilder.heading: Warum Ericeira für deine Surf Villa wählen?

children.text: Bei uns erlebst du mehr als nur eine Unterkunft – unsere Villa ist ein Treffpunkt für internationale Surfer, die Luxus und authentischen Surf Lifestyle verbinden möchten. Die entspannte Atmosphäre entsteht durch die richtige Mischung aus Privatsphäre und Community.
Yoga Sessions
(dreimal pro Woche) helfen dir, nach intensiven Surf-Sessions zu regenerieren und flexibel zu bleiben. Unsere
Wine & Cheese Abende
bringen Gäste aus aller Welt zusammen – perfekt zum Austausch von Wave-Stories und neuen Freundschaften.

Unsere Philosophie: Surfen und Luxus gehören zusammen. Du musst nicht auf Komfort verzichten, um die besten Wellen Europas zu surfen. Bei uns bekommst du beides – hochwertige Unterkünfte, erstklassige Surfspots und eine Community, die deine Leidenschaft teilt.

children.text: Ericeira ist eine charmante Fischerstadt, deren weiß getünchte Häuser sich an die Klippen über dem Atlantik schmiegen. Nur 35 Kilometer von Lissabon entfernt, bietet die Region perfekte Ausflugsmöglichkeiten: Die magischen Paläste von Sintra, das pulsierende Leben der Hauptstadt oder die wilden Strände der Costa da Caparica sind schnell erreicht.

Im Ort selbst findest du authentische Restaurants mit frischem Fisch, gemütliche Cafés und lokale Surfshops. Der Wochenmarkt bietet regionale Produkte, und abends erwacht das Zentrum mit Live-Musik und entspannter Atmosphäre. Skateparks, Wanderwege entlang der Klippen und versteckte Buchten laden zu Aktivitäten abseits des Surfens ein.
